results:
In this study, two types of levofloxacin-loaded nanoparticles (LVX-NPs) were prepared using two different polymers, i.e., Poly lactic-co-glycolic Acid (PLGA) and low molecular weight chitosan (CS).
3.1. UV Spectra, encapsulation efficiency, and drug loading capacity of nanoparticles
The drug loading capacity (LC), was estimated via LVX calibration curve (Fig. 2A). The LC of LVX-CS-NPs; CS-I, CS-II and CS-III was 52.2% +- 0.2%, 66.86% +- 0.17%, and 82.65% +- 0.36, respectively. The LC of PLGA-I and PLGA-II formulations was 86% +- 0.21 and 52.41 +- 0.45, respectively (Table 1).
Abbreviations: CS; chitosan, PLGA; poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid), nm: nanometer, EE; encapsulation efficiency, LC; loading capacity, PVA; polyvinyl alcohol
UV spectra obtained showed the presence of LVX in polymeric PLGA and CS NPs (Fig. 2B), with maximum absorbance at 288 nm. Among various LVX-CS-NPs, the CS-III formulation exhibited a maximum encapsulation of 87.47%+- 0.08, while CS-I exhibited the lowest encapsulation of 57.14%. +- 0.03 The entrapment efficiency (EE) of PLGA-I and PLGA-II nanoparticles was 42.8% +- 0.65% and 23.08% +- 0.4, respectively (Table 1).
3.2. Size and polydispersity index of nanoparticles
As shown in Table 1, NPs synthesized using a 1:1 ratio of LVX and CS (CS-I) demonstrated the smallest particle size of 559 nm, followed by the particle size of 595 nm and 758 nm for 1:2 (CS-II) and 1:3 (CS-III), respectively. The particle sizes of LVX-PLGA-I (PVA1%) and LVX-PLGA-II (PVA2%) were 92 nm and 313 nm, respectively, keeping the LVX-PLGA ratios constant (Table 1). The polydispersity index (PI), a measure of heterogeneity in a sample based on size, was 0.030 and 0.057 for CS-1, and CS-II, respectively, and 0.058 for PLGA-I, suggesting more of a mono-dispersion system compared to 0.1 for CS-III and 0.076 for PLGA-II (Table 1).
3.3. Fourier transfer infrared (FTIR) spectra
FTIR spectra were performed to determine characteristic peaks of a pure drug in synthesized nanoparticles.
3.4. FTIR spectra of LVX-CS-NPs
FTIR spectra of free-levofloxacin (LVX), CS polymer, sodium tripolyphosphate, and LVX-CS-NPs; CS-I, CS-II, and CS-III are documented in Table 2. The LVX spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks at 3265.cm-1 due to COOH (aromatic), 2933 cm-1 due to C-CH3 (C-methyl group), 1722 cm-1 due to CO i.e., carbonyl group, 1295 cm-1 due to C-N group and 1088 cm-1 due to Fluoro group (Fig. 2C). The FTIR spectra of chitosan and blank chitosan nanoparticles showed characteristic peaks at 3358 cm-1 linked to an amino group (NH2) and a hydroxyl group (OH) stretching vibration, while the peaks at 1636 cm-1 and 1526 cm-1 showed the presence of CONH2 and NH2 groups (Table 2). These peaks were shifted from 1647 cm-1 and 1558 cm-1 in CS-blank-NPs probably due to the interaction of the NH3 group of chitosan and phosphate group of tripolyphosphate. Another significant peak in CS-blank-NPs was at 1220.7 cm-1 showing the presence of phosphate group stretching vibration (Fig. 2D). The FTIR spectra of CS-I, CS-II, and CS-III showed the characteristic peak of both CS-NPs and a drug (LVX) in Fig. 2E. When the IR bands of levofloxacin, chitosan and LVX-CS nanoparticles were compared, a marked shift was observed in the intensity of the bands of LVX-CS-NPs compared to CS-blank-NPs, reflecting the ionic-interaction between chitosan and levofloxacin (Fig. 2D&E) (Table 2).
3.4.1. FTIR spectra of LVX-PLGA-NPs
FTIR spectra of LVX-PLGA-NPs did not show major shifts in the peaks. The intensity of bands representing the characteristic peaks remained similar in both reference and LVX-PLGA-NPs (Fig. 2F-H). These data demonstrated that the functional groups were intact with drug encapsulation in PLGA, thereby, the drug characteristic peaks had less intensity than PLGA (Table 3).
3.5. X-ray diffractometry (XRD)
The XRD analysis was performed for LVX-CS-NPs and LVX-PLGA-NPs. The X-ray diffraction of a pure drug showed crystalline shapes of free-LVX and sharp peaks, characteristic of LVX crystallinity . Whereas, the chitosan showed two characteristic peaks as 2 = 10o and 20o. . The PLGA is amorphous, so no crystallinity peak was observed .
The XRD patterns of LVX-CS-NPs, CS-I, CS-II, CS-III, and LVX-PLGA-NPs, PLGA-I & PLGA-II, are shown in Fig. 3A & B. In both cases, reduced spectral peaks were observed. The comparative patterns of both LVX-PLGA-NPs and LVX-CS-NPs are shown in Fig. 3C. These patterns indicated the amorphous nature of both PLGA and Chitosan-based LVX nanoparticles.
3.6. In-vitro drug release study
In-vitro drug release studies were performed and the free-LVX release was compared with all the formulations; CS-I, CS-II, CS-III, PLGA-I, and PLGA-II (Fig. 3D) (Table S1). Polymeric-Levofloxacin nanoparticles showed the biphasic release pattern, initially, there was a rapid discharge of the drug in the solution followed by the sustained release of LVX. The initial burst in CS-I, CS-II, and CS-III showed LVX release of 25.06%, 33.96%, and 24.21%, respectively (Table S1). After 24 h, LVX release was 40.47%, 43.97%, and 48.65% for CS-I, CS-II, and CS-III, respectively. While, after 120 h, the LVX release was 61.21%, 58.44%, and 51.81% for CS-I, CS-II, and CS-III, respectively (Fig. 3D) (Table S1).
3.7. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
Scanning electron microscopy was performed to determine the morphology of PLGA and chitosan-based levofloxacin nanoparticles at a scale of 10-100 um.
3.7.1. SEM study of LVX-CS-NPs
The SEM data showed that LVX-CS-NPs, CS-I, CS-II & CS-III, had irregular surfaces but few were seen as round with smooth surfaces, though with some agglomeration (Fig. 4A-Ci-ii), which might happen during ion-sputtering with the gold.
Morphologically, the LVX-CS-NPs appeared dense cubical, rectangular, and spherical. They were attached, yet retained their shape. The addition of surfactants can improve the morphology of nanoparticles - probably more spherical (Fig. 4A-Ci-ii).
3.7.2. SEM analysis of LVX-PLGA-NPs
The SEM analysis showed that LVX-PLGA-NPs, PLGA-I, and PLGA-II, had smooth circular surfaces but were agglomerated. There is a possibility that agglomeration might happen during the ion-sputtering with the gold (Fig. 4D-Ei-ii).
3.8. Stability study
The stability data of CS-I, CS-II & CS-III, and PLGA-I and PLGA-II are shown in Table 4. Data suggested that there were no significant changes in the drug content starting from 1st month till 6th month - the difference was even less than 3% (Table 4). Likewise, no significant changes were observed in the color in the 1st, 3rd and 6th months (Table 4). The aggregation was observed in LVX-PLGA-NPs with passing time.
3.9. Antibacterial study
The bacteria were isolated from the patient's blood, urine, and tracheal samples having high-grade fever and raised c-reactive proteins (CRP) following their admission to Ittefaq Hospital (trust), Lahore. The samples were cultured and isolates were identified the same day via Versatrek automated microbial identification system and Vitek 2 identification system.
Only CS-III and PLGA-I were selected for their antibacterial activity based on their characteristics, such as encapsulation efficiency, drug loading, etc. Data suggested that CS-III exhibited higher anti-bacterial activity except for Klebsiella pneumonia with an inhibition zone of 18 mm compared to 13 mm in PLGA-I samples (Fig. 5A-C, Table 5). Furthermore, against all gram-negative bacteria, CS-III demonstrated greater anti-bacterial activity in terms of zone of inhibition compared to PLGA-I and free-LVX. While, against gram-negative bacteria, almost similar zones of inhibitions were observed for both the formulations except for Klebsiella, which showed even less sensitivity against CS-III compared to free-LVX, i-e., 14 mm, whereas, the breakpoint of sensitivity should be equal to or greater than 17 mm as per CLSI guidelines, yet, the PLGA-I showed much better zone of inhibition i.e. 18 mm (Fig. 5A-C, Table 5).
Abbreviations: MRSA; methicillin resistant Staphylococcus aureus, LVX; levofloxacin, CS; chitosan, mm; millimeter, ug; micrograms
PLGA-1 and CS-III were selected based on their characteristics, i-e., encapsulation efficiency, loading capacity and stability etc. p-values; 0.05 - 0.002 * , <= 0.001 * *
3.10. Toxicological study
For toxicological studies, PLGA-I and CS-III formulations along with control and free-LVX were tested against freshly isolated human red blood cells (RBCs, n = 4). The PLGA-I and CS-III were incubated with RBCs for 1 h at 37oC (Fig. 6A & B).
The RBCs in the control and free-LVX samples demonstrated no morphological changes, i-e., the RBCs retained their spherical shapes with smooth surfaces (Fig. 6A & B). Likewise, the PLGA-I treated RBCs showed no changes in the morphology, no interaction with the surface of RBCs and no hemolysis (Fig. 6C).
Similarly, the CS-III samples demonstrated no vivid morphological changes in RBCs, yet the nanoparticles adhered to the surface of erythrocytes, probably due to their positive ion. The RBCs retained their shapes but the CS-III adhered with RBCs. Also, no hemolysis was observed with CS-III treatment (Fig. 6D).
discussion:
Despite, several available options for antimicrobial therapy, the global increase in antimicrobial resistance is threatening the population worldwide.  With the number of new antibiotics egregiously limited, the rising antibiotic resistance poses a continuous threat to confront infectious diseases . Thus, the dwindled antibiotic conduits initiated a changeover in research stratagems to configure the existing antibiotic molecules, such as antibiotic functionalized nanoparticles and pure antibiotics engineered into nanoscale having potential for targeted delivery , . The present study demonstrated the synthesis, characterization, and antimicrobial sensitivity of LVX-NPs against resistant bacterial strains employing two different FDA-approved polymers, i-e., PLGA and Chitosan (CS). Three different CS-based, CS-I, CS-II & CS-III, and two different PLGA-based polymeric NPs, PLGA-I and PLGA-II, were prepared and characterized for the particle size, physiochemical nature, morphology, polydispersity, crystallographic structure and drug release profiles. Data revealed that CS-III and PLGA-I demonstrated the highest entrapment efficiency (EE) and loading capacity (LC), while CS-I and PLGA-I had smaller particle sizes. Both the formulations, CS-III and PLGA-I, had optimal drug release profiles and stability over 6 months, and were found effective against gram-positive and gram-negative resistant bacterial strains compared to free-LVX.
The composition of PLGA and CS nanoparticles was investigated by FTIR analysis. Both formulations showed the characteristic peaks of the drug and a polymer. However the few peaks of the drug were masked and were reduced in intensity possibly due to the entrapment property of the polymer. ,  The SEM and XRD analysis were carried out to determine the morphology and nature of prepared polymeric nanoparticles. The SEM of LVX-PLGA-NPs showed a smooth circular surface but few were found agglomerated due to their very small size. The nano-size increases the surface area and the interaction forces among the particles which results in agglomeration. On the other hand, the LVX-CS-NPs had irregular surfaces but few were round with smooth surfaces. Both CS and PLGA NPs demonstrated some agglomeration, which may be due to ion-sputtering with the gold . The morphology of LVX-CS-NPs were seen as dense cubical, rectangular, and spherical. The XRD patterns of both polymeric nanoparticles showed the amorphous nature, as no characteristic peak of LVX was observed, and the drug is completely interacted and encapsulated in the polymeric matrix .
The utility spectrum of NPs is determined by morphological characteristics, such as particle size, polydispersity index, zeta potential, surface charge, and shape. These properties affect NP's biological activities, while preparation methods play a pivotal role in controlling these properties. Our data suggested that a constant ratio of LVX, yet with increasing CS ratio (1:3) resulted in large size NPs (CS-III) and increased PDI, indicative of broad particle size distribution, and higher EE and LC. While out of three formulations, CS-I (1:1) with equal ratios exhibited the smallest particle size, and monodispersing system but with lower EE and LC, corroborating previous findings , . There have been conflicting reports with regards to the effect of mass ratios of CS and a drug on NP characteristics, e.g., particle size decreases with increasing concentration of CS , , but with subsequent increase and decrease  in EE%. The explanation of these differences compared to data presented in this study could be the cross-linker concentration, which was not maneuvered, and the negligible contribution of the loading drug (LVX) as an ionic crosslinker. However, Zhang and Hu used Alphastatin, which acted as a polyanionic cross-linker with cationic CS , demonstrating enough ionic interaction that resulted in small particle size and high EE%, nonetheless, the reduced EE% in our case could be attributed to intermediate lipophilicity of LVX . However, the encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity of the CS-I (1:1) were among the lowest of all three formulations.
The previous report clearly demonstrated that the PLGA and PVA concentrations have a direct impact on particle size of the NPs  prepared using the double emulsion-solvent evaporation method. However, it has also been shown that changing PLGA concentration does not have any significant effect on the PDI and particle size of NPs prepared using the double emulsion evaporation method.  Thus, we examined only the effects of surfactant (PVA) concentration on NP characteristics keeping the drug (LVX) and PLGA concentrations constant. The change in PVA concentrations can be argued based on previous findings that the increasing concentration of PVA guarantees better stabilization, optimal size, and reduced coalescence of emulsion , while increasing the concentration of PVA above 2.5% has been argued to exist as an aggregated form rather than a single molecule.  We observed that by changing the concentrations of PVA, the particle size and PDI of LVX-PLGA-NPs increased with increasing PVA concentration, while the EE and LC decreased significantly. The increase in particle size can be attributable to the higher viscosity of the aqueous phase affecting the sonication shear stress that might result in the formation of large emulsion droplets. While PVA concentration of more than 1% has been shown to reduce the EE and LC . Seemingly, the 0.5% PVA is considered as the most optimum concentration to attain higher EE and LC, corroborating previous findings,which may be attributable to higher viscosity and large but fewer emulsion droplets. .
Additionally, particle size and charge have been shown to impact EE and drug release.  We observed that by increasing CS concentration the drug release decreased from 61% to 51% with a decrease in particle size, though the pattern was bi-phasic. The drug release from CS NPs is governed by polymer swelling, loaded drug per se, diffusion, erosion, and degradation of the polymer. Therefore, the main mechanism of LVX release, a hydrophilic drug, could be diffusion and polymer erosion and degradation, while higher drug release of CS-I over 120 h could be attributable to reduced particle size which has been shown to exhibit larger initial burst release and optimal polymer swelling, erosion and degradation compared to CS-II. The slower drug release from CS-II or CS-III, due to increased polymer ratio, could be due to several factors, such as polymer swelling, polymer erosion or degradation and diffusion of drug from the polymer. Notably, drug diffusion from the matrix can be described by Fick's law of diffusion - initial burst followed by slow release, attributable to diffusion barrier by polymeric chains associated with swelling or erosion . While the swelling of the polymer matrix occurs via non-Fick's diffusion system, affected by relaxation constant - higher value of the relaxation constant, slower will be the drug release from the matrix . When it comes to PLGA, it has been reported that initial drug release from the PLGA matrix was via diffusion followed by the release of a drug by both diffusion and degradation of a polymer.  We observed that PLGA-I formulation (PVA1%) demonstrated improved drug release of ~88.58% over 120 h compared to PLGA-II (PVA2%) of 82.6% - corroborating previous report that cumulative drug release was higher for the PLGA-NPs synthesized using 1% PVA compared to 2% PVA. . Other plausible explanations could be the higher PVA concentration  and impact of particle size on release kinetics, as small particles may have greater initial burst release due to surface absorption and the capacity of entrapment .
The antibacterial potential of LVX-PLGA-NPs has been reported earlier with the slow release of LVX, yet having reduced antibacterial effects compared to free-LVX.  Contrary to this report, compared to free-LVX (17-19 mm), we found that both CS-III (22-24 mm) and PLGA-I (22-24 mm) demonstrated higher average zone of inhibition (24-26 mm) against resistant gram-negative E. coli, S. aureus, MRSA and S. pneumonia. The differences can be explainable by higher EE of 43% by PLGA-I compared to 30% reported by Lopez-Lopez et al. . However, similar to our findings, CIP-CS-NPs has shown dose dependent improvements in antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus . Moreover, compared to free-LVX (14-17 mm), against resistant gram-positive bacteria, P. aeruginosa and K. pneumonia, the average zone of inhibition of LVX-PLGA-I was 18-21 mm while it was 13-21 mm for LVX-CS-III - showing improved antibacterial activity by PLGA-NPs. However, compared to PLGA-1, only K. pneumonia demonstrated reduced sensitivity against CS-III, which in the case of PLGA-I, may be attributable to better and sustained drug release profiles as argued before regarding antibiotic slow-release profiles, , . Additionally, both PLGA-I and CS-III were found non-toxic, as no hemolysis was observed in human red blood cells (RBCs), nonetheless, CS-III showed increased binding to the surface of RBCs, which might interfere with the functioning of RBCs or may be RBC's function may be altered while carrying nanoparticles..
conclusion:
Taken together, these data suggested that among various formulations of LVX-NPs, CS-III and PLGA-I demonstrated the highest loading capacity and entrapment efficiency, and with a significant effect of polymer (CS) and surfactant (PVA), on particle size. The polydispersity was optimal for all the formulations - mono-dispersion system, except for CS-III and PLGA-II. Major shifts in FTIR spectra were observed for CS-NPs due to the interaction of NH3 and phosphate groups, while no shifts were observed for PLGA-NPs. SEM data suggested spherical shapes of CS-NPs and circular shapes of PLGA-NPs with some agglomeration. With formulations having an amorphous nature and stable for up to 6 months, the highest in vitro drug release patterns were observed for CS-I and PLGA-I after 120 h. CS-III, having a higher polymer ratio, demonstrated better antibacterial activity against resistant strains except against K. pneumonia whereas PLGA-I, having 1% PVA, was found superior against K. pneumonia.

materials_methods:
The Levofloxacin hemihydrate used as a drug sample was gifted by Neon Chemicals Ltd. (batch no. DC-004-1911001). All the chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade; Low molecular weight Chitosan 50,000- 190,000-Dalton (Sigma Aldrich, CAS#9012-76-4 lot# STBH6262), Sodium tripolyphosphate (Sigma Aldrich, CAS#72061 lot#BCCC2990), Sodium Hydroxide (Merck, Germany) and Glacial Acetic Acid (Sigma Aldrich, USA), PLGA, or Poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) 50:50 IV 1.0 dl/g (Polysciences, Inc. USA. Lot# A777168), Polyvinyl Alcohol, the average molecular weight of 30,000 to 70,000 Dalton (Sigma Aldrich, USA) and Dichloromethane (Reagents, Duksaw). PBS tablets (Sigma Aldrich, USA) and levofloxacin discs (Oxoid, Catalog No. CT1587B).
Muller Hilton Agar for sensitivity test (Oxoid, CM0337). Dialysis tubing (12,000-14,000 molecular weight), and Medicell Membranes Ltd. Identification discs (Blank) were purchased from Abtek Biologicals Ltd, UK. The commercially available levofloxacin discs (Oxoid, Catalog No. CT1587B) were used as reference standards.
Six species of resistant bacteria were isolated from the patient samples of blood, urine, and tracheal aspirate gifted by the Ittefaq Hospital Trust, Lahore to carry out the antibacterial studies. Gram-negative bacteria included Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Klebsiella pneumonia. Gram-positive bacteria included Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumonia and Methicillin Resistant Staphylococcus aureus, MRSA.
For toxicological studies, washed peripheral red blood cells (PRBCs) were obtained from the blood bank of Ittefaq Hospital Trust, Lahore.

2.2.2. Synthesis of chitosan-based nanoparticles
2.2.2.1. Levofloxacin loaded chitosan (LVX-CS) nanoparticles
The different composition of LVX and CS for the preparation of LVX-CX-NPs are summarized in Table S1. Different concentrations of Chitosan gel solution (i.e. 2 mg/mL, 4 mg/mL and 6 mg/mL) were prepared by dissolving the chitosan in 1% glacial acetic acid. These solutions were subjected to continuous stirring overnight by magnetic stirrer at 300-400 rpm speed until a clear gel solution was obtained. The resultant solution pH was adjusted at 4.7 to 5.0 by dropwise adding 1 M NaOH. The chitosan solution was then filtered with syringe filter having pore size 0.45 um. To aforementioned prepared gel solution of different concentrations, constant amount of LVX was added in three different ratios, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 and stirred for 1 h. The aqueous solution of sodium tripolyphosphate was prepared in accordance with the concentration of polymer i.e. 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3. The sodium tripolyphosphate solution was added dropwise into the drug-chitosan solutions at rate of 12 mL/hour with vigorous stirring, 700-800 rpm, at room temperature to obtain CS-I, CS-II and CS-III. These solutions were subjected to probe sonication for 5 min with amplitude of 40%. The suspensions were then homogenized on magnetic stirring for 2 h to evaporate the organic solvent. The resultant final suspensions (CS-I, CS-II and CS-III) were then subjected to ultracentrifugation at 12000 rpm for 40 mins. Later, the collected pellets from supernatant were washed with deionized water to remove free drug from pellets. The supernatants were then analyzed further to determine the drug entrapment efficiency. These pellets were again suspended in deionized water by magnetic stirring and then ultracentrifuged at speed of 12000 rpm for 40 min. The washing process repeated in triplicate to completely remove the excess free drug.
The resultant pellets were then suspended in deionized water and freeze dried at 0.4mbar and - 40oC for 24 h. This lyophilized levofloxacin loaded chitosan nanoparticles of different concentrations were stored at 2-4oC for further characterization. The two more batches were prepared with same abovementioned technique to determine the reproducibility of the method. , , , . The schematic diagram of LVX-CS-NPs synthesis I s shown in Fig. 1A.
2.2.3. Synthesis of PLGA-based nanoparticles
The PLGA nanoparticles were prepared with and without loading the levofloxacin by double emulsion-solvent evaporation technique .
2.2.3.1. Levofloxacin-loaded PLGA (LVX-PLGA) nanoparticles
LVX-PLGA nanoparticles were prepared using varying the concentration of PVA (1% and 2%), while keeping levofloxacin and polymer ratios constant, i-e., 1:2 (Table S2) (Fig. 1B).
Briefly, PLGA was dissolved in 10 mL DCM by vortexing. Levofloxacin was added in 6 mL of PVA solution with vigorous stirring until a clear solution. Next, 5 mL of the organic phase was added drop-wise in the aqueous phase on a magnetic stirrer containing drug and stabilizer PVA to form an oil-water (o/w) emulsion. The emulsion is subjected to probe sonication for 90 s at 40% amplitude. The resultant o/w emulsion was added dropwise into the remaining 5 mL organic phase with constant stirring to form a double emulsion and re-sonicated under the same conditions. This double emulsion was added drop-wise in PVA solution to stabilize the double emulsion followed by sonication for 5.5 min at 40% amplitude. The organic solvent was placed on a magnetic stirrer for overnight evaporation. Pellets were obtained by centrifugation, washed with deionized water, and suspended again in deionized water followed by centrifugation for 40 min at 12000 rpm. The centrifugation and washing were done twice to remove the free drug. The supernatant was stored The obtained levofloxacin-loaded PLGA nanoparticles were freeze-dried at 0.4 m bar and - 40oC for 24 h. The nanoparticles were stored at 2-4oC. The schematic diagram of LVX-PLGA-NPs synthesis I s shown in Fig. 1B.
2.3. Characterization
2.3.1. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR)
The FTIR spectra were obtained for the following; free-LVX, polymers; CS, PLGA, blank PLGA-NPs & CS-NPs, levofloxacin loaded PLGA nanoparticles (LVX-PLGA-NPs) and chitosan nanoparticles (LVX-CS-NPs) via Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer (Benchtop FTIR instrument). All spectra were obtained by scanning the samples in IR from 400 to 4000 cm-1 range with 8 cm-1 spectra resolution and beam size of 10-100 um. A total three scans were obtained for each sample and the best one was chosen for comparison. The spectra of free-LVX, CS, CS-NPs, and LVX-CS-NPs were compared, likewise, spectra of free-LVX, PLGA, PLGA-NPs, and LVX-PLGA-NPs were compared.
2.3.2. Percentage entrapment efficiency (EE)
The percentage entrapment efficiency of the prepared NPs, LVX-CS and LVX-PLGA, were directly determined by dissolving known mass of both lyophilized LVX-CS-NPs and LVX-PLGA-NPs in 5 mL of their respective solvents and centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 40 min. The amount of incorporated drug in the precipitates of the centrifuged nanosuspension was measured via UV-visible spectrophotometer at 288 nm wavelength. Thereafter, using previously obtained typical standard curve  the percentage entrapment efficiency (EE%) of LVX in CS and PLGA was determined using following formula(1.1)
2.3.3. Percentage drug content
The drug content study was performed as described previously . In summary, using a UV spectrophotometer the amount of LVX loaded onto CS and PLGA-NPs was quantified by comparing it with standard curve of free (standard) levofloxacin.
Determination of drug content of levofloxacin loaded chitosan nanoparticles.
5 mg of LVX-CS-NPs were dissolved in 5 mL of 1% glacial acetic acid with continuous magnetic stirring for 12 h. The collected supernatant was assayed in triplicates by a UV spectrophotometer at 288 nm. Absorbance was noted and concentration was determined with a reference calibration curve of standard levofloxacin. % Drug content was determined by the following formula in the equation(1.2)
2.3.3.1. Determination of drug content of levofloxacin-loaded PLGA nanoparticles
5 mg of LVX-PLGA-NPs were dissolved in 5 mL of DCM and 5 mL of deionized water with continuous magnetic stirring for 8 h. The collected supernatant was assayed in triplicates by a UV spectrophotometer at 288 nm. Absorbance was recorded and concentration was determined with the reference calibration curve of standard levofloxacin. % Drug content was determined by the drug content formula Eq. 1.2.
2.3.4. Particle size analysis
The particle size of LVX-NPs (PLGA and Chitosan-based) was determined by using a BT-90 Nano laser particle size analyzer. The required quantity of nanoparticles was dissolved in deionized water and sonicated on a bath sonicator for 30 min to reduce particle adhesion. This solution is diluted to maintain the photon number in the range of 20-40 K. The sample was run for 3 min at 30oC. Six measurements were taken for each sample and the average of those values (standard deviation) were considered.
2.3.5. Scanning electron microscope-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX)
The surface morphology of LVX-NPs was observed via scanning electron microscopy. It was carried out on SEM (model S-3400) operated at 10-15KV voltage. The Samples were placed directly onto the SEM by double-sided sticking tape and then coated with ion sputter-gold spray. Micro-images were obtained on different resolutions from 10-100 um.
2.3.6. X-ray diffraction patterns
The patterns of X-ray diffraction were determined for polymeric LVX-NPs. XRD measurements were taken by exposing the samples in monochromatic nickel-filtered copper radiations in an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker).
2.3.7. In-vitro dissolution profile
The in-vitro dissolution study of polymeric LVX-NPs was carried out using dissolution apparatus USP II with dialysis tubing method to determine the sustained release pattern of both types of nanoparticles i.e. LVX-CS-NPs and LVX-PLGA-NPs. Briefly, the dialysis membrane was pre-treated with distilled water for 4 h to remove glycerol and placed overnight in a buffer solution, pH 7.4 (Sodium chloride NaCl 0.137 M and potassium chloride KCl 0.0027 M) at 25oC.
In a plastic tube, 5 mg of levofloxacin-loaded nanoparticles equivalent to 5 mg of LVX with 2 mL PBS solution were added, the base of which was enclosed with a presoaked dialysis membrane. The dialysis tubing system was affixed on a paddle in a separate dissolution flask containing 90 mL dissolution medium at pH 7.4 and 37oC temperature. The sink conditions were maintained by dissolving of NPs in fresh medium time to time, ensuring adequate volume of dissolution medium and removing the dissolved drug by partitioning it . The paddles were rotated at 100 rpm for 72 h. Next, 4 mL of the medium was drawn from each vessel according to pre-determined time intervals i.e., 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h, 36 h, 48 h, and 72 h. The obtained media were then centrifuged and filtered before assay using a UV spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 288 nm. An equal quantity of fresh PBS was added back to the dissolution medium to replace the lost fluid. .
The dissolution release rate curve was determined by the following formulas(1.3)(1.4)(1.5)
2.3.8. Stability studies
Stability studies were conducted to determine the stability of LVX-NPs by keeping them in amber glass bottles wrapped with aluminum foil and kept in an incubator at 37 degC and 75% relative humidity (RH) for 6 months. The changes in appearance and drug content were investigated after 1 month, 3 months and 6 months.
2.4. Biological assay
2.4.1. Biological samples
The blood samples of febrile patients were collected in Versatrek Redox 1 (aerobic) and Redox 2 (anaerobic). The samples of febrile patients were collected included urine, tracheal swabs, and wound swabs. The samples were inoculated with the help of a needle/inoculating loop on agar culture media plates under a laminar flow hood and were incubated for 72 h at 37oC. The plates were examined after every 24 h. The collecting colonies were identified by using VITEK 2 identification (ID) GN, by uploading directly and after adjusting the turbidity of colony suspension (greater than 0.5 McFarland). , .
2.4.2. In-vitro antibacterial studies
The antibacterial activity of Blank, free-LVX, LVX-CS-NPs, and LVX-PLGA-NPs against gram-negative; Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Klebsiella pneumonia, gram-positive bacteria; Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumonia and Methicillin Resistant Staphylococcus aureus, MRSA was carried out via Kirby-Bauer Disk Diffusion susceptibility test method employing Muller Hinton agar.  The bacterial strains of both gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria were spread on different culture media, such as blood base, DNASE agar, MacConkey, Cled medium with Andrade indicator, chocolate, and Muller Hinton agar using sterile cotton swabs.
Sterile blank discs were placed in petri dishes 5 mm apart. By using a micropipette, a fixed volume of 10uL (containing levofloxacin) was loaded on each disc in a laminar flow hood. After drying 10 mL solution was again instilled on each disc and dried at 37oC for 4 h, and later transferred to a sterile air-tight container followed by storage in a freezer at - 20oC with CaCl2 as a desiccant.

.

2.2.1. Synthesis of blank nanoparticles

The biological samples demonstrating positive growth of bacterial species were selected for further antibacterial susceptibility studies. The prepared Muller Hinton plates were inoculated with isolated colonies by streak method. The desired discs i.e. impregnated with LVX-PLGA-NPs, LVX-CS-NPs, and commercially available free-levofloxacin were placed in the plates with the help of a needle. The commercial antibiotic discs were used as standard and the prepared nanoparticles were loaded onto blank discs. Plates were incubated for the next 24 h. After 24 h of incubation, the zone of inhibition was measured with a measuring scale in mm for each disc. .
2.4.2.2. Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)
The MIC of LVX-CS-NPs and LVX-PLGA-NPs was determined using the standard broth serial microdilution method. The bacterial inoculums were adjusted to the concentration of 6 x 105 CFU/mL. For every experiment negative control (medium only) and positive control (medium and inoculum) were included in 96-well micro-well plates. In each well medium and test compounds were added and serially diluted followed by the addition of bacterial inoculum and incubation at 37 degC for 18 - 24 h. The absorbance of the wells was read at 570 nm using microtiter plate reader. The lowest concentration of antibacterial agent that inhibits bacterial growth was considered as MIC value.
2.4.3. In-vitro toxicological studies
The in vitro toxicological studies were conducted to determine the safety margin of prepared polymeric nanoparticles, LVX-CS, LVX-PLGA - 50 mg/mL, 500 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, and 5 mg/mL in EP glass vial for 1 h at 37oC. The cytotoxic effects were estimated by employing human erythrocytes. The fresh human blood was obtained from Blood Bank Ittefaq Hospital Trust, Lahore. The red blood cells (RBCs) were incubated in PBS (control), with free-LVX, LVX-CS, and LVX-PLGA, all in triplicates. The treatment effects of nanoparticles were compared with the control concerning erythrocyte morphology and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) .
2.4.4. Statistical analysis
Data were analyzed using GraphPad Prism version 9. Significant differences among the groups were estimated using one way ANOVA. An alpha value to 0.05 or more was considered statistically significant.
